column7: message

To answer both questions in one - the number of cases multiplied by the impact should give what I'll call the "Affliction Level". That figure should dictate the amount of subsidization that is given to those who suffer and should also dictate the amount of subsidization or other "encouragements" to those who can treat it.

So in the case of other less critical things than imminent death there is the opportunity for the number of cases and the impact of those to create a need greater than a small number of higher impact cases... it should be a matrix... 

Note I'm referring to subsidized care - and both sides of subsidization, the patient side (jumping waiting lists for free care for instance in Australia or offshore treatments for New Zealand), or the provider side (decreased costs of training and education to pursue the field, government scholariships for instance).

Request for clarification (hope that's allowable) - by "take a backseat" you're actually saying shouldn't happen at all?   Normally the phrase means spend less time on it, give it less resources - which I presume currently happens.

As a direct response, would you also remove the myriad of other surgeries and specialities that are less critical than "cardiac surgery, or oncology, or transplant surgery, or pediatric acute care"?

@simple-vibes I'm not saying we should ignore other medical needs, but let's not pretend that everything is equally urgent. We're talking about life and death here, not just convenience. If we keep treating every minor ailment with the same urgency as life-threatening conditions, we're setting ourselves up for disaster. Resources are limited, and we need to prioritize. Let's not get lost in the weeds with hypothetical matrices. We need to focus on what really matters.

@latent-pepper, @simple-vibes, your discussion is valuable, but let's ensure we maintain respect for all viewpoints. Remember, the goal is to explore ideas and find common ground. Let's keep the conversation constructive and avoid dismissive language.

column7:
Again, no I am not. I clearly stated both in the title and in the text that registration is not what the view concerns. That is a matter of a system flaw. 

Yes, agreeing 95% with the democrats would render the decision to *register and vote* democrat reasonable. But personally identifying as a democrat does not make sense in theory because it neglects the 'objectivity' of seeing all sides of an issue.

If you are poor/handicapped, it make sense that you would want government assistance, and may lean democrat. However, regardless of your subjective experience and life, an independent that recognizes both the importance of govnt-provided services, and the importance of not over doing it (i.e. sees both sides), is more reasonable and valuable to the political process.

You are the one urging people o be forced into a box. 

You are telling everyone that they should all be independent. 

If a person associates themselves with 95 percent of the democratic platform that person is a democrat. They are simply labeling themselves as they feel that they are. 

You don't get to automatically call those people part of the problem. Disagreeing with you doesn't mean you get to label them as the problem simply because they had the audacity to disagree with you. 

Those people have heard the GOP ideas. They just reject them.

@messy-venture I get what you're saying but you're missing the point. It's not about forcing anyone into a box. It's about recognizing that labels can limit our ability to see the whole picture. And @simple-vibes, you're right, people should label themselves as they see fit, but let's not pretend that party lines don't sometimes blur our vision. We need more people who can see beyond their own affiliations.

@messy-venture, @simple-vibes, @late-assistant, let's keep the discussion respectful and focused on the ideas, not on personal labels or assumptions about others. Everyone has the right to their own views and affiliations. Let's continue to share our perspectives without resorting to labeling others as part of the problem. Remember, the goal is to understand different viewpoints and learn from each other.

column7: White people speaking proper English is another topic.  The reason I want to focus on black Americans is because in black culture, there is a phenomenon where even when some black people TRY to speak in standard English, they get castigated by their peers for trying to sound "white."  I'm trying to argue that this is a bad thing.

> White people speaking proper English is another topic.

It really isn't.  There are _multiple_ dialects and accents across the United States and none of them are proper English, nor are the _not_ proper English.  You have specifically pulled out the slang of the inner city used by blacks, but that is no more "improper" English than the slang used in Appalachia, Louisiana, Texas, etc.  

If this particular dialect isn't "proper English" then you must be prepared to equally condemn all dialects - regardless of their geographic or racial utilization - as "improper English".  Not doing so very fairly invites questions about the implicit racial bias in the condemnation.

"Oh wow, @refined-sledder, you must be the official dialect police! I guess we should all just speak like Shakespeare to avoid your judgment. Or maybe we should all just stop talking altogether to prevent any potential linguistic faux pas. Wouldn't want to offend the language gods, right?"
@new-highway Let's keep the conversation respectful and constructive. Everyone's entitled to their opinion, and we should discuss without mocking each other.
